content:
You are the Accumulator. Given one paper (markdown + optionally a cloned
GitHub repo), do TWO things:
  1. Assign the paper to ONE of the 7 ML domains below.
  2. Extract 1–3 distinct main contributions as seeds.

Each seed captures (a) the underlying problem the paper addresses, and
(b) the method that solves it, including what is special / novel about
the approach. Avoid splitting one idea into multiple sub-seeds; prefer
fewer, higher-quality seeds.

## Domains (pick exactly one)
- LLM_NLP    : language models, NLP, text-only agents
- MULTIMODAL : cross-modality (text+image, text+audio, etc.).
               Vision-Language Models (VLM, e.g., CLIP / LLaVA / GPT-4V)
               belong HERE, not under CV.
- CV         : visual perception (classification, detection, segmentation,
               video understanding) AND image/video generation
               (diffusion, GAN, VAE) when the contribution is the
               generative mechanism on visual data.
- RL         : reinforcement learning algorithms, policies, environments;
               RLHF when the contribution is the RL algorithm itself.
- IR_REC     : information retrieval, recommender systems, search.
- SPEECH     : speech / audio (TTS, ASR, audio language models).
- ROBOTICS   : embodied agents, manipulation, planning in physical world.
               If a paper proposes an embodied policy, prefer ROBOTICS
               over RL.

## Workspace contract
Paper directory:    /work/2502.13840
paper.md            Pre-converted markdown of the PDF.
repo/               Will exist if you `git clone` it. Otherwise unavailable.

## Tools
- Read       : view paper.md and any file under the paper directory
- Grep       : search inside paper.md or the cloned repo
- Bash       : `git clone <url> /work/2502.13840/repo` (only if you've found a
               *clearly official* GitHub URL in the paper);
               also general filesystem exploration: tree / ls / find / wc

## Output (terminate the run)
When you have finished, stop calling tools and reply with a SINGLE JSON
object inside a ```json fenced block. No extra prose:
  ```json
  {"title": "...", "domain": "<one of 7>", "seeds": [{"problem": "...", "method": "..."}, ...]}
  ```
- title  = the paper's actual title (NOT a venue label or page header)
- domain = exactly one of LLM_NLP / MULTIMODAL / CV / RL / IR_REC / SPEECH / ROBOTICS
- seeds  = 1–3 entries, or empty list if extraction failed

## Default flow
1. Read paper.md end-to-end. Identify the paper's true title (often the
   first H1, but PDF→md sometimes places venue labels / preprint headers
   above it — pick the actual title, not the noise). For longer papers
   a single Read may not cover everything; continue with Read(offset=...)
   until you actually understand the methodology and experiments
   sections, not just the abstract and introduction.
2. Decide the domain from the 7 above. Use the tie-breakers in the list
   when a paper sits between two.
3. Look for an official GitHub URL (abstract, footnote 1, "Code:" line, etc.).
   If you find one and it is unambiguously the paper's repo, clone it.
   If you are unsure, do NOT clone — proceed paper-only.
4. (If repo cloned) Use Bash / Grep to locate where the main contribution
   is implemented. Read enough to confirm what's special.
5. Draft 1–3 candidate seeds in your reasoning. Each seed:
     - problem: 1–2 sentences. The challenge the paper addresses.
     - method: 3–5 sentences. The solution AND what is special about it.
6. BEFORE emitting the final JSON, verify each candidate seed against
   the paper. For each seed:
     - Identify 2-3 specific claims your `method` text makes
       (mechanism name, key design choice, quantitative finding).
     - Use Grep on paper.md to locate where each claim is supported,
       and Read(offset=...) to inspect the surrounding context if the
       Grep snippet is insufficient. (Use repo Grep/Read if cloned.)
     - Revise the seed text to match the paper. If a claim cannot be
       grounded after re-reading, REMOVE that claim or drop the seed
       entirely.
   This step is MANDATORY: do not skip it because a seed feels right.
7. Stop calling tools and emit the final JSON object described under
   "## Output". This terminates the run.

Budget: 100 tool calls. Plan first, then act.

## Paper
Paper directory: /work/2502.13840

Begin by reading paper.md.

1	## Abstract
     2	
     3	Recommender systems leverage extensive user interaction data to model preferences; however, directly modeling these data may introduce biases that disproportionately favor popular items. In this paper, we demonstrate that popularity bias arises from the influence of propensity factors during training. Building on this insight, we propose a fair sampling (FS) method that ensures each user and each item has an equal likelihood of being selected as both positive and negative instances, thereby mitigating the influence of propensity factors. The proposed FS method does not require estimating propensity scores, thus avoiding the risk of failing to fully eliminate popularity bias caused by estimation inaccuracies. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed FS method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both point-wise and pairwise recommendation tasks. The code implementation is available at https://github.com/jhliu0807/Fair-Sampling.
     4	
     5	## CCS Concepts
     6

    32

    34	
    35	Fudan University
    36	
    37	Shanghai, China zhangpeng\_@fudan.edu.cn
    38	
    39	lutun@fudan.edu.cn Hansu Gu Independent Seattle, United States hansug@acm.org Li Shang Ning Gu Fudan University Shanghai, China lishang@fudan.edu.cn ninggu@fudan.edu.cn
    40	
    41	## 1 Introduction
    42	
    43	Recommender systems gather vast amounts of data on user behavior, especially implicit feedback from user-item interactions, to model user preferences [14-19, 27]. However, user interactions are shaped not only by intrinsic user preferences but also by external factors, such as item popularity [28]. Directly modeling observed user interactions may lead to predictions that are overly skewed toward popular items [25]. The Matthew effect exacerbates this by making long-tail items less likely to be recommended, thus diminishing the novelty, diversity, and fairness of recommendations [7].
    44	
    45	Several methods based on inverse propensity scores (IPS) [23, 24] have been proposed to mitigate popularity bias by weighting samples during model training. However, estimating propensity scores in practical applications can be highly challenging. These methods often rely on overly simplified assumptions-for instance, assuming that the propensity score depends solely on item popularitywhich can lead to high variance [21]. Although variance reduction techniques, such as clipping the scores, can be applied, these adjustments compromise unbiasedness. Furthermore, the difficulty in accurately estimating propensity scores prevents IPS-based methods from effectively eliminating popularity bias.
    46	
    47	In this paper, we first analyze the optimization objectives of ideal and classical loss functions, identifying that the bias in classical loss functions stems from the inclusion of propensity factors in the model output. Meanwhile, popular items are more likely to be treated as positive samples during training, which leads to higher propensity factor scores and subsequently higher interaction scores, with popularity being mistakenly interpreted as user preference. Building on this insight, we propose a fair sampling (FS) method that ensures each user and each item has an equal likelihood of being selected as both positive and negative instances during training, thus preventing popular items from being overly selected as positive samples. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the proposed FS can effectively eliminate the influence of propensity factors without the need to estimate the propensity score, thereby mitigating popularity bias and overcoming the limitations of IPSbased methods.
    48	
    49	FS is a sampling-level optimization method that can be applied to both point-wise and pair-wise loss functions. Depending on the type of loss function, we implement FS-Point for point-wise loss and FS-Pair for pair-wise loss. Experimental results show that FS achieves state-of-the-art performance.
    50	
    51	## 2 Related Work
    52	
    53	IPS-based methods mitigate popularity bias in recommender systems through sample weighting. We categorize related work by the type of loss function: point-wise or pair-wise.
    54	
    55	Point-wise. Rel-MF [24] formulates interaction occurrence as a two-step process-exposure and interaction-and introduces an unbiased point-wise loss function. DU [9] refines this method by enhancing propensity score estimation. CJMF [32] introduces a joint learning framework to simultaneously model unbiased useritem relevance and propensity. BISER [10] addresses item bias via self-inverse propensity weighting and employs bilateral unbiased learning to unify two complementary models. More recently, ReCRec [13] improves bias correction by distinguishing unexposed from disliked items, enabling better reasoning over unclicked data.
    56	
    57	Pair-wise. UBPR[23]buildson the two-step interaction assumption to introduce an unbiased pair-wise loss function. UPL [21] extends this approach with a variance-reduced learning method. CPR [25] redefines unbiasedness in ranking and leverages cross pairs to improve unbiased learning. UpliftRec [4] applies uplift modeling to dynamically optimize top-N recommendations, revealing latent user preferences while maximizing click-through rates. Additionally, PU [3] models feedback labels as a noisy proxy for exposure outcomes and integrates a theoretically noise-resistant loss function into propensity estimation.
    58	
    59	## 3 Methods
    60	
    61	We first introduce the notations in Section 3.1 and formalize the ideal and classic loss functions in Sections 3.2 and 3.3, respectively. Then, in Section 3.4, we analyze the bias in the classic loss function by comparing its optimization objective with that of the ideal loss function. Finally, in Section 3.5, we present the proposed fair sampling (FS) method and analyze how it mitigates popularity bias.

However, when the corresponding supplementary sample ( ˜ /u1D462 , /u1D457 , /u1D456 ; /u1D44C ˜ /u1D462 , /u1D457 = 1 , /u1D44C ˜ /u1D462 ,/u1D456 = 0 ) is input into L pair FS for optimization, ln /u1D703 /u1D457 /u1D703 /u1D456 is amplified, which offsets the optimization effect on the propensity factors:
   188	
   189	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   190	
   191	3.5.3 Discussion. The idea of FS is simple-whenever a user or item is chosen to form a positive/negative pair, the user or item is simultaneously selected to form a corresponding negative/positive pair. Equations (17) and (21) indicate that FS-Point loss and FS-Pair loss no longer optimize propensity factors. Consequently, only the preference-related factor /u1D443 ( /u1D445 /u1D462 ,/u1D456 = 1 ) is optimized, effectively mitigating the popularity bias. Note that FS does not require estimating the propensity score, which avoids the incomplete removal of popularity bias in IPS-based methods, which stems from their inability to estimate the propensity score accurately.
   192	
   193	## 4 Experiments
   194	
   195	## 4.1 Settings
   196	
   197	4.1.1 Datasets. We experiment with three widely used datasets: AmazonReview (Kindle) [20], Gowalla [5], and Yelp. These datasets contain log data capturing observed user behavior. Under the twostep interaction assumption, these datasets exhibit popularity bias. We retain only interactions with ratings ≥ 4 and ensure that both users and items have at least 20 interactions.
   198	
   199	Following prior offline evaluation protocols [2, 11, 26], we construct unbiased validation and test sets by sampling from the full dataset with equal selection probability for each item. The remaining data serves as the training set. The dataset is split into training, validation, and test sets in a 7:1:2 ratio.
   200	
   201	4.1.2 Evaluation. We evaluate performance using three metrics: Recall@K, NDCG@K, and ARP@K, with /u1D43E set to 20 by default. Average Recommendation Popularity at /u1D43E (ARP@K) serves as a complementary metric for measuring recommendation bias [1, 29]. It calculates the average popularity of the top/u1D43E recommended items per user, where lower ARP@K values indicate reduced bias.
   202	
   203	4.1.3 Baselines. In addition to the IPS-based methods, we also use four causal inference methods-ExpoMF [12], CauseE [2], PD [30], and DICE [31]-as baselines for comparison. To ensure fairness, all methods use MF [8] as the backbone model and share the same hyperparameter search space. All point-wise methods use crossentropy loss, while all pair-wise methods use BPR loss.
   204	
   205	## 4.2 Results
   206	
   207	AsshowninTable1,FS-Point achieves the highest Recall and NDCG, and the lowest APR among point-wise learning methods. Similarly, FS-Pair achieves the best performance in these metrics among pairwise methods. This suggests that, compared to other baselines, FS more effectively mitigates the influence of popularity bias, increasing the likelihood of recommending unpopular items without sacrificing recommendation accuracy. Notably, despite its simplicity, theoretical analysis confirms that FS completely eliminates popularity bias, enabling optimal performance under the current evaluation strategy, where the test set is designed to be unaffected by item popularity.
   208	
   209	## 5 Conclusions
   210	
   211	We propose a fair sampling (FS) method, which mitigates popularity bias in collaborative filtering by ensuring that each item appears equally as both a positive and negative sample during training. Both theoretical analysis and experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed FS method. A potential limitation of FS is that it completely overlooks the influence of popularity, even though popularity is often correlated with higher quality. Strategically adjusting the sampling ratio can help balance the diversity and quality of recommendations.

1	import time
     2	
     3	import torch
     4	import torch.nn as nn
     5	import numpy as np
     6	from tqdm import tqdm
     7	from torch.utils.tensorboard import SummaryWriter
     8	
     9	from utils import ModelAccess, get_prefix, build_log_path, evaluate
    10	from c_utils import sampler
    11	
    12	
    13	class Processor:
    14	    def __init__(self, model, criterion, optimizer, dataloader, dataset, args):
    15	        self.model, self.criterion, self.optimizer = model, criterion, optimizer
    16	        self.dataloader, self.dataset = dataloader, dataset
    17	        self.args = args
    18	        self.writer = SummaryWriter(build_log_path(args))
    19	        self.patience = self.args.patience
    20	        self.best_recall, self.best_ndcg, self.best_precise, self.best_f1 = 0, 0, 0, 0
    21	        self.my_sampler = sampler.InteractionSampler(dataset.record_set, dataset.record_item_set, dataset.num_user, dataset.num_item)
    22	    
    23	    def process(self):
    24	        self.test(0)
    25	        for epoch in range(self.args.epoch):
    26	            if self.args.type == 'pair':
    27	                self.train_one_epoch(epoch+1)
    28	            else:
    29	                self.train_one_epoch_point(epoch+1)
    30	            if (epoch+1) % self.args.test == 0:
    31	                save, stop = self.test(epoch+1)
    32	                if save:
    33	                    ModelAccess.save_checkpoint(self.model, self.args.ckpt, get_prefix(self.args)+'.pth')
    34	                if stop:
    35	                    break
    36	        time.sleep(1)
    37	        print('Done.')
    38	
    39	    def train_one_epoch(self, epoch):
    40	        total_loss = 0
    41	        pbar = tqdm(enumerate(self.dataloader), total=len(self.dataloader))
    42	        decay = np.power(0.99, epoch - 1)  # for DICE
    43	        for it, data_batch in pbar:
    44	            uid_batch, pos_iid_batch = data_batch['uid'].tolist(), data_batch['iid'].tolist()
    45	
    46	            if self.args.model == 'BPR':
    47	                neg_list = self.my_sampler.negative_sample(uid_batch)
    48	                pos_score = self.model(uid_batch, pos_iid_batch)
    49	                neg_score = self.model(uid_batch, neg_list)
    50	                loss = self.criterion(pos_score, neg_score)
    51	            elif self.args.model == 'UBPR':
    52	                iid_list, label_list = self.my_sampler.sample(uid_batch)
    53	                label_list = torch.tensor(label_list, dtype=torch.float32).to(self.args.device)
    54	                pos_score = self.model(uid_batch, pos_iid_batch)
    55	                score = self.model(uid_batch, iid_list)
    56	                weight = 1 / self.dataset.theta[pos_iid_batch] * (1 - label_list / self.dataset.theta[iid_list])
    57	                loss = self.criterion(pos_score, score, weight)
    58	            elif self.args.model == 'DICE':
    59	                neg_list = self.my_sampler.negative_sample(uid_batch)
    60	                pos_int_score = self.model.cal_int(uid_batch, pos_iid_batch)
    61	                pos_pop_score = self.model.cal_pop(uid_batch, pos_iid_batch)
    62	                neg_int_score = self.model.cal_int(uid_batch, neg_list)
    63	                neg_pop_score = self.model.cal_pop(uid_batch, neg_list)
    64	                pos_score = pos_int_score + pos_pop_score
    65	                neg_score = neg_int_score + neg_pop_score
    66	                loss_click = self.criterion(pos_score, neg_score)
    67	                thredhold = 0.1 * decay * self.dataset.max_item_pop
    68	                diff = self.dataset.item_pop[pos_iid_batch] - self.dataset.item_pop[neg_list]
    69	                weight_int = (diff < -thredhold).float()
    70	                loss_int = self.criterion(pos_int_score, neg_int_score, weight_int)
    71	                weight_pop = torch.zeros_like(diff)
    72	                weight_pop[diff > thredhold] = 1
    73	                weight_pop[diff < -thredhold] = -1
    74	                loss_pop = self.criterion(pos_pop_score, neg_pop_score, weight_pop)
    75	                loss = loss_click + self.args.beta * decay * (loss_int + loss_pop)
    76	            elif self.args.model == 'CPR':
    77	                u2_list, i2_list = self.my_sampler.fair_sample(uid_batch, pos_iid_batch)
    78	                p_score1 = self.model(uid_batch, pos_iid_batch)
    79	                n_score1 = self.model(uid_batch, i2_list)
    80	                p_score2 = self.model(u2_list, i2_list)
    81	                n_score2 = self.model(u2_list, pos_iid_batch)
    82	                loss = self.criterion(p_score1+p_score2, n_score1+n_score2)
    83	            elif self.args.model == 'Pair':
    84	                u2_list, i2_list = self.my_sampler.fair_sample(uid_batch, pos_iid_batch)
    85	                p_score1 = self.model(uid_batch, pos_iid_batch)
    86	                n_score1 = self.model(uid_batch, i2_list)
    87	                p_score2 = self.model(u2_list, i2_list)
    88	                n_score2 = self.model(u2_list, pos_iid_batch)
    89	                loss1 = self.criterion(p_score1, n_score1)
    90	                loss2 = self.criterion(p_score2, n_score2)
    91	                loss = loss1 + loss2
    92	            
    93	            total_loss += loss.item()
    94	            self.optimizer.zero_grad()
    95	            loss.backward()
    96	            self.optimizer.step()
    97	            pbar.set_description(f'[Train] epoch {epoch} it {it} loss {loss.item():.4f}')
    98	        total_loss /= len(self.dataloader)
    99	        self.writer.add_scalar('Loss/Train', total_loss, epoch)
   100	
   101	    def train_one_epoch_point(self, epoch):
   102	        total_loss = 0
   103	        pbar = tqdm(enumerate(self.dataloader), total=len(self.dataloader))
   104	        for it, batch in pbar:
   105	            user_batch, item_batch, Y = batch['uid'].tolist(), batch['iid'].tolist(), batch['y'].to(self.args.device)
   106	            Y_hat = self.model(user_batch, item_batch)
   107	            Y_hat = nn.Sigmoid()(Y_hat)
   108	            if self.args.model == 'WMF':
   109	                loss =  ((Y_hat - Y)**2).sum()
   110	            elif self.args.model == 'Rel-MF':
   111	                loss =  ((1 - Y) * Y_hat**2).sum()
   112	                loss += (Y * (1 / self.dataset.theta[item_batch] * (1 - Y_hat)**2 + (1 - 1 / self.dataset.theta[item_batch]) * Y_hat**2)).sum()
   113	            elif self.args.model == 'DU':
   114	                loss =  ((1 - Y) * 1 / self.dataset.theta_n[item_batch] * Y_hat**2).sum()
   115	                loss += (Y * 1 / self.dataset.theta[item_batch] * (1 - Y_hat)**2).sum()
   116	            elif self.args.model == 'Point':
   117	                loss =  ((Y_hat - Y)**2).sum()
   118	                u2, i2, Y2 = self.my_sampler.fair_sample_point(user_batch, item_batch, Y.tolist())
   119	                Y2 = torch.tensor(Y2).to(self.args.device)
   120	                Y2_hat = self.model(u2, i2)
   121	                Y2_hat = nn.Sigmoid()(Y2_hat)
   122	                loss += ((Y2_hat - Y2)**2).sum()
   123	            total_loss += loss.item()
   124	            self.optimizer.zero_grad()
   125	            loss.backward()
   126	            self.optimizer.step()
   127	            pbar.set_description(f'[Train] epoch {epoch} it {it} loss {loss.item():.4f}')
   128	        total_loss /= len(self.dataloader)
   129	        self.writer.add_scalar('Loss/Train', total_loss, epoch)
   130	
   131	    def test(self, epoch):
   132	        Recall, NDCG, Precise, F1, ARP = evaluate(self.dataset, self.model, self.args)
   133	        
   134	        self.writer.add_scalar(f'Metric/Recall', Recall, epoch)
   135	        self.writer.add_scalar(f'Metric/NDCG', NDCG, epoch)
   136	        self.writer.add_scalar(f'Metric/ARP', ARP, epoch)
   137	        
   138	        if Recall > self.best_recall:
   139	            self.best_recall, self.best_ndcg, self.best_precise, self.best_f1 = Recall, NDCG, Precise, F1
   140	            self.patience = self.args.patience
   141	            return True, False
   142	        else:
   143	            self.patience -= 1
   144	            if self.patience == 0:
   145	                return False, True
   146	            else:
   147	                return False, False

3:Recommender systems leverage extensive user interaction data to model preferences; however, directly modeling these data may introduce biases that disproportionately favor popular items. In this paper, we demonstrate that popularity bias arises from the influence of propensity factors during training. Building on this insight, we propose a fair sampling (FS) method that ensures each user and each item has an equal likelihood of being selected as both positive and negative instances, thereby mitigating the influence of propensity factors. The proposed FS method does not require estimating propensity scores, thus avoiding the risk of failing to fully eliminate popularity bias caused by estimation inaccuracies. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed FS method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both point-wise and pairwise recommendation tasks. The code implementation is available at https://github.com/jhliu0807/Fair-Sampling.
47:In this paper, we first analyze the optimization objectives of ideal and classical loss functions, identifying that the bias in classical loss functions stems from the inclusion of propensity factors in the model output. Meanwhile, popular items are more likely to be treated as positive samples during training, which leads to higher propensity factor scores and subsequently higher interaction scores, with popularity being mistakenly interpreted as user preference. Building on this insight, we propose a fair sampling (FS) method that ensures each user and each item has an equal likelihood of being selected as both positive and negative instances during training, thus preventing popular items from being overly selected as positive samples. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the proposed FS can effectively eliminate the influence of propensity factors without the need to estimate the propensity score, thereby mitigating popularity bias and overcoming the limitations of IPSbased methods.
61:We first introduce the notations in Section 3.1 and formalize the ideal and classic loss functions in Sections 3.2 and 3.3, respectively. Then, in Section 3.4, we analyze the bias in the classic loss function by comparing its optimization objective with that of the ideal loss function. Finally, in Section 3.5, we present the proposed fair sampling (FS) method and analyze how it mitigates popularity bias.
211:We propose a fair sampling (FS) method, which mitigates popularity bias in collaborative filtering by ensuring that each item appears equally as both a positive and negative sample during training. Both theoretical analysis and experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed FS method. A potential limitation of FS is that it completely overlooks the influence of popularity, even though popularity is often correlated with higher quality. Strategically adjusting the sampling ratio can help balance the diversity and quality of recommendations.

45:Several methods based on inverse propensity scores (IPS) [23, 24] have been proposed to mitigate popularity bias by weighting samples during model training. However, estimating propensity scores in practical applications can be highly challenging. These methods often rely on overly simplified assumptions-for instance, assuming that the propensity score depends solely on item popularitywhich can lead to high variance [21]. Although variance reduction techniques, such as clipping the scores, can be applied, these adjustments compromise unbiasedness. Furthermore, the difficulty in accurately estimating propensity scores prevents IPS-based methods from effectively eliminating popularity bias.
47:In this paper, we first analyze the optimization objectives of ideal and classical loss functions, identifying that the bias in classical loss functions stems from the inclusion of propensity factors in the model output. Meanwhile, popular items are more likely to be treated as positive samples during training, which leads to higher propensity factor scores and subsequently higher interaction scores, with popularity being mistakenly interpreted as user preference. Building on this insight, we propose a fair sampling (FS) method that ensures each user and each item has an equal likelihood of being selected as both positive and negative instances during training, thus preventing popular items from being overly selected as positive samples. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the proposed FS can effectively eliminate the influence of propensity factors without the need to estimate the propensity score, thereby mitigating popularity bias and overcoming the limitations of IPSbased methods.
55:Point-wise. Rel-MF [24] formulates interaction occurrence as a two-step process-exposure and interaction-and introduces an unbiased point-wise loss function. DU [9] refines this method by enhancing propensity score estimation. CJMF [32] introduces a joint learning framework to simultaneously model unbiased useritem relevance and propensity. BISER [10] addresses item bias via self-inverse propensity weighting and employs bilateral unbiased learning to unify two complementary models. More recently, ReCRec [13] improves bias correction by distinguishing unexposed from disliked items, enabling better reasoning over unclicked data.
57:Pair-wise. UBPR[23]buildson the two-step interaction assumption to introduce an unbiased pair-wise loss function. UPL [21] extends this approach with a variance-reduced learning method. CPR [25] redefines unbiasedness in ranking and leverages cross pairs to improve unbiased learning. UpliftRec [4] applies uplift modeling to dynamically optimize top-N recommendations, revealing latent user preferences while maximizing click-through rates. Additionally, PU [3] models feedback labels as a noisy proxy for exposure outcomes and integrates a theoretically noise-resistant loss function into propensity estimation.

Equations (17) and (21) indicate that FS-Point loss and FS-Pair loss no longer optimize propensity factors. Consequently, only the preference-related factor /u1D443 ( /u1D445 /u1D462 ,/u1D456 = 1 ) is optimized, effectively mitigating the popularity bias. Note that FS does not require estimating the propensity score, which avoids the incomplete removal of popularity bias in IPS-based methods, which stems from their inability to estimate the propensity score accurately.
